content.text: Overview

Human-as-Reviewer is the pattern where the agent completes a full task — writes a document, generates code, drafts an email — and then presents its output to a human for review before committing it to the real world. Unlike a HITL gate that approves individual actions, the reviewer evaluates the whole output at once: they accept it as-is, request specific edits and ask the agent to revise, or reject it entirely. The agent loops through revisions until the output is accepted or a maximum revision count is reached.

attrs.alt: Iterative review loop: agent produces draft, human accepts, edits (agent revises), or rejects; edit cycle repeats up to max_revisions

content.text: When to Use This Pattern

Human-as-Reviewer is appropriate when correctness and quality of the full output matters more than speed, and when reviewing the finished result is faster than approving individual steps. Writing tasks are the canonical example: reviewing a drafted email takes 30 seconds; approving each word-placement decision would be impractical. The pattern also applies to code generation, plan creation, report writing, and any task where the output is a coherent artifact rather than a sequence of independent actions.
Use case
What the reviewer evaluates
Email or document drafting
Tone, accuracy, completeness, formatting
Code generation
Correctness, security, style, edge-case handling
Plan or proposal creation
Feasibility, risk, alignment with constraints
Data transformation or extraction
Accuracy against source, coverage of required fields
Customer-facing content
Brand voice, accuracy, regulatory compliance
Core Data Structures

The review pattern requires two core types: a Draft record that carries the artifact through versions, and a ReviewFeedback record that captures the reviewer's decision and edit requests in a structured form the agent can act on.
The Review Loop

The agent loop runs inside a revision outer loop. After each execution the artifact is wrapped in a Draft, persisted, and handed to the reviewer. On an edit decision the feedback is injected back into the conversation as a user message and the agent produces a revised draft. On accept or reject the loop terminates.

import asyncio, anthropic, json

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

async def run_with_review(
    task:         str,
    tools:        list[dict],
    produce_draft: callable,   # (agent_output) -> Any — extract the reviewable artifact
    commit:        callable,   # (draft) -> Any — finalize the draft in production
    task_id:      str | None = None,
    max_revisions: int = 3,
) -> Any:
    """
    Run the agent, produce a draft output, wait for human review.
    If the reviewer requests edits, revise and re-submit (up to max_revisions).
    Returns the committed output on accept, None on reject.
    """
    import uuid
    task_id = task_id or str(uuid.uuid4())
    messages = [{'role': 'user', 'content': task}]
    revision_of = None

    for attempt in range(1, max_revisions + 1):
        # Step 1: Run the agent to produce an output
        agent_output = await run_agent(messages, tools)

        # Step 2: Extract the reviewable artifact from the agent output
        artifact = produce_draft(agent_output)

        # Step 3: Create a draft record
        draft = Draft.create(task_id, artifact, version=attempt, revision_of=revision_of)
        _drafts[draft.draft_id] = draft
        event = asyncio.Event()
        _review_events[draft.draft_id] = event

        # Step 4: Notify reviewer and wait
        await notify_reviewer(draft)
        await event.wait()   # set by the /review/{draft_id} endpoint

        feedback = draft.feedback
        if feedback.decision == ReviewDecision.ACCEPT:
            return commit(draft)

        if feedback.decision == ReviewDecision.REJECT:
            return None

        # EDIT: build revision prompt and loop
        edit_context = (
            f'Your previous draft was reviewed. The reviewer requests these changes:\n'
            + '\n'.join(f'- {item}' for item in feedback.edit_items)
            + (f'\n\nAdditional notes: {feedback.comments}' if feedback.comments else '')
            + f'\n\nPlease produce a revised version.'
        )
        messages.append({'role': 'user', 'content': edit_context})
        revision_of = draft.draft_id

    # Max revisions reached without acceptance
    raise RuntimeError(f'Task {task_id} not accepted after {max_revisions} revisions')

async def run_agent(messages: list[dict], tools: list[dict]) -> str:
    while True:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-8",
            max_tokens=2048,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )
        messages.append({'role': 'assistant', 'content': response.content})
        if response.stop_reason == 'end_turn':
            return next(b.text for b in response.content if b.type == 'text')
        results = []
        for block in response.content:
            if block.type == 'tool_use':
                result = {'status': 'ok'}  # actual dispatch here
                results.append({'type': 'tool_result', 'tool_use_id': block.id, 'content': json.dumps(result)})
        messages.append({'role': 'user', 'content': results})

async def notify_reviewer(draft: Draft) -> None:
    print(f'[review] Draft {draft.draft_id} v{draft.version} ready for review')
Review API Endpoints
The reviewer accesses the draft through a read endpoint and submits their decision through a write endpoint. The write endpoint wakes the waiting agent coroutine by setting the asyncio event.
Showing the Diff Between Revisions

When the agent produces a revised draft, the reviewer should see not only the new draft but also what changed from the previous version. A unified diff makes it immediately clear which parts of the document were altered in response to the edit request.
Structuring Reviewer Feedback

Free-form comments are ambiguous for the agent. Structured feedback — location of the issue, a description of the problem, and a suggested fix — gives the agent actionable revision instructions and produces more targeted edits in fewer revisions.
Preventing Revision Loops
Set max_revisions (typically 2–3): if the agent cannot satisfy the reviewer in three attempts, the task probably needs more than revision — re-start with a better task description

Pass the full history of feedback on each revision: if the agent only sees the latest edit request it may fix the new issue but re-introduce a previously fixed one; include all prior feedback in the revision prompt

Distinguish edit from reject early: if the reviewer would reject the draft regardless of revisions (wrong task, wrong audience, confidential information included), reject immediately rather than iterating
Log every revision cycle: each attempt with its feedback and the resulting revision is a fine-tuning signal — capture draft_id, version, feedback, and the revised content
Reviewer Fatigue and Checklist Design

When the same reviewer evaluates many drafts per day they develop fatigue and begin rubber-stamping without reading carefully. Mitigate this by providing a structured review checklist alongside the draft — a short list of specific criteria the reviewer should verify. A checklist converts an open-ended 'does this look right?' into a concrete series of yes/no questions that take 30 seconds to complete.
Integrating Review Outcomes as Training Signal

Every review cycle is a labelled example: the draft is the model output, the decision is the label, and the edit items are the correction. Persist this data in a structured format and use it to improve the agent's system prompt, to write new few-shot examples, or as the basis for a fine-tuning dataset.
Summary

Human-as-Reviewer decouples task execution from task approval. The agent runs the full task, produces a draft, and blocks until a human has evaluated the complete output. The reviewer accepts, edits, or rejects. On edit the structured feedback is injected back into the conversation and the agent revises in a controlled loop bounded by max_revisions. Structured feedback — with location, issue, and suggestion per edit item — produces better revisions in fewer iterations than free-form comments. The diff view between versions helps reviewers confirm their requested changes were made correctly.
